pathophysiology_summary: Candida overgrows in warm, moist, macerated intertriginous skin, favoured here by recent broad-spectrum antibiotics (loss of competing flora), poorly controlled diabetes (glucose-rich environment, impaired immunity) and a large body habitus with deep folds. The result is confluent beefy-red erythema with maceration and, characteristically, satellite papules and pustules beyond the main border. Discriminators: tinea cruris typically spares the scrotum/deep creases, has an active scaly advancing border with central clearing and no satellite pustules; erythrasma is a mild brown-red finely scaling patch (coral-red on Wood's lamp); inverse psoriasis is chronic, smooth, sharply demarcated and non-pustular. Management is a topical antifungal (± short low-potency steroid for inflammation), meticulous drying/barrier care, and addressing precipitants (glycaemic control), with a safety-net for secondary bacterial cellulitis (higher risk in diabetes).

differential.evidence_basis: Beefy-red macerated fold erythema with satellite pustules after antibiotics in a diabetic.
Groin rash, but tinea has an active scaly edge with central clearing and no satellite pustules.
Erythrasma is mild brown-red fine scaling, not acute beefy pustular inflammation.
Usually chronic, smooth, sharply demarcated and non-pustular.
Diabetic with broken skin — screen for spreading induration, pus and fever.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Recognise candidal intertrigo from satellite pustules/pattern and screen for secondary cellulitis; address precipitants, don't just prescribe a cream.

differential.evidence_basis: Satellite pustules, beefy erythema, antibiotic/diabetes precipitants; afebrile with no spreading induration.
Satellite pustules and involvement of deep creases argue against tinea.
Afebrile, no spreading induration or purulence — remains a safety-net.
Working diagnosis: topical antifungal + drying/barrier care, optimise diabetes, cellulitis safety-net.

red_flags.description: Secondary bacterial cellulitis: fever, spreading hard/hot erythema beyond the folds, thick purulent exudate
red_flags.clinical_significance: In a diabetic with broken skin, secondary cellulitis can progress rapidly and needs antibiotics ± admission. Absent now.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to cellulitis/sepsis in a diabetic.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Missing satellite pustules and using an antifungal choice/framing appropriate to tinea, or a potent steroid alone worsening candida.
Prescribing a cream without addressing moisture, antibiotics and glycaemic control.
Not screening/safety-netting for secondary bacterial infection in a diabetic.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Prescribe topical antifungal (± short low-potency steroid), give drying/barrier and glycaemic advice, and safety-net for cellulitis; in-person/urgent review if infection develops.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Using a potent topical steroid alone (worsens candida), or missing early secondary cellulitis in a diabetic.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Candidal intertrigo: topical imidazole (e.g., clotrimazole), keep areas dry, consider a short course of a low-potency topical corticosteroid for inflammation, and treat predisposing factors.
